info.title: getting started
info.text: In this demo we'll use BlazePose, an open source machine learning model by Google (part of the MediaPipe framework), to detect 33 body keypoints in real time from your webcam. We'll use the position of your eyes to scale and place an emoji right on your face like an AR filter.

info.title: libraries
info.text: We're importing four libraries. The first is the familiar nn library. The other three download the functions necessary for running BlazePose in your browser: the core TensorFlow.js and accompanying libraries.

info.title: global vars
info.text: We declare three variables at the top level: model will hold the loaded AI model, video will be the webcam feed element, and emoji will be the DOM element we move over the face. Making them global keeps them accessible across both the setup and update functions.

info.title: the start button
info.text: Rather than running immediately on page load, we create a button with the nn library and wire it to call setup on click. This matters because requesting webcam access, which happens inside setup, requires a user gesture in all modern browsers. The button provides that gesture.

info.title: webcam video
info.text: The setup function is async because it needs to await a few things. The first is creating a video element and feeding it a webcam stream using nn.askFor(), which prompts the browser's camera permission dialog and returns a MediaStream. We set autoplay and muted so the video plays immediately and silently, and playsinline to prevent mobile browsers from going full-screen.

info.title: the emoji element
info.text: We create a div element containing a sunglasses emoji and .scale(0) to shrink it to nothing so it doesn't appear until a face is detected. Once the update loop finds a pose it will scale and position this element over the face each frame.

info.title: loading the model
info.text: Next we load the pose-detection model, first by specifying the type, then a config object. Setting runtime: 'mediapipe' tells the library to use the native MediaPipe WASM runtime (faster than the pure TensorFlow.js one). The solutionPath points to the same CDN URL as our import on line 3, so the model weights load from there. Lastly, .createDetector() downloads and initializes everything, storing the result in our model variable.

info.title: start the loop
info.text: After everything is initialized, this.remove() deletes the start button from the page (this keyword refers to the button because that's what called setup()), and then update() is called once to kick off the render loop.

info.title: the update loop
info.text: The update function is also async because it awaits the model's inference (aka prediction) each frame. Calling requestAnimationFrame(update) tells the browser to call update recursively, roughly 60 times per second. This creates a continuous loop that keeps the face tracking live.

info.title: estimatePoses
info.text: We run model.estimatePoses(), passing it the current video frame. The model returns an array of detected poses, each representing a possible person in the frame. We grab the first one with poses[0].

info.title: eye keypoints
info.text: If a pose is detected, we extract two keypoints from its keypoints array: index 2 is the left eye and index 5 is the right eye. Each keypoint has an x and y property giving its pixel coordinates on the video frame. The full 33 point skeleton covers the entire body, so you could track shoulders, wrists, hips and more.

info.title: scale and position
info.text: We use nn.dist() to get the pixel distance between the two eyes, which naturally grows as the face gets closer to the camera and shrinks as it moves away. nn.map() then remaps that distance from the range 0–video.width to 0–150, giving us a scale value we pass to emoji.scale(). We also call .position(left.x, left.y) to anchor the emoji to the left eye each frame.

info.title: lots more
info.text: This demo uses just two of the 33 available keypoints to place a single emoji. With the full skeleton you could detect body posture, measure joint angles, or trigger events when someone waves or raises their arms. Face tracking is just one of the open source TensorFlow.js models you can run locally in the browser, keeping data private on-device and reducing the need for energy-intensive datacenter processing.
